caption: Figure 1: Examples of various segmentation tasks on heterogeneous document datasets.
llm_description: This image displays eight distinct datasets used for document analysis tasks, each labeled with its name and visual example: PubLayNet (layout analysis), MTHv2 (historical documents), SCUT-CAB-Logical (historical documents), CTW1500 (scene text detection), M⁶Doc (table detection/recognition), cTDaR-Modern (table detection/recognition), cTDaR-Archive (table detection/recognition), SCUT-HCCDoc (handwritten documents), and Total-Text (scene text detection).

caption: Figure 2: Network structure of the proposed DocSAM. DocSAM unify various document image segmentation tasks into one single model through instance and semantic query decomposition and interaction. Skip connections and norm layers are omitted for simplicity.
llm_description: This diagram illustrates a vision-language model architecture for document understanding, featuring pre-processing of text and images, a vision encoder (Vision Backbone + Deformable Attention), and a multi-scale decoder with hybrid query mechanisms. The model generates predictions including mask, class, and bounding box outputs from combined visual and semantic embeddings.

caption: Figure 3: Loss curves and on-the-fly validation during training.
llm_description: Two comparative plots show training loss (left) and on-the-fly validation mAP (right) across 20,000 iterations for DocSAM-base variants: baseline, with curriculum learning, SimMIM pretraining, Sentence-BERT training, masked attention, query interaction, query selection, and a larger model. Both charts reveal that curriculum learning and SimMIM pretraining yield the lowest training loss and highest validation mAP, indicating superior performance over other modifications.

caption: Figure 4: Qualitative results on public document layout analysis benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: Ten document layout datasets displayed side-by-side, each showing diverse page structures with color-coded blocks highlighting text, images, and tables, labeled below as BaDLAD, CDLA, D4LA, DocBank, DocLayNet, ICDAR2017-POD, IIIIT-AR-13K, M6Doc, PubLayNet, and RanLayNet.

caption: Figure 5: Qualitative results on public ancient and handwritten document segmentation benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 10 labeled images showcasing diverse historical document layouts, each annotated with colored bounding boxes for text region detection, representing various datasets used in Chinese historical document layout analysis.

caption: across a wide range of document processing tasks, highlighting its potential for practical applications in various domains.
Figure 6: Qualitative results on public table detection and structure recognition benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 16 labeled images showcasing diverse table recognition datasets and benchmarks, including FinTabNet, PubTabNet, ICDAR competitions, cTDaR variants, NTTable series, TableBank formats, TNCB, STDW, and WTW — each displaying unique table structures, layouts, and OCR challenges for AI training.

caption: Figure 7: Qualitative results on public scene text detection benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 20 diverse real-world images showcasing text in varied contexts — from storefronts, billboards, and signs to packaging and handwritten documents — each labeled with its corresponding dataset name, illustrating the challenge of recognizing text across languages, fonts, orientations, and backgrounds.

caption: Figure 8: Failure cases produced by our DocSAM model. “GT” means ground-truth and “DT” means detection results.
llm_description: This image compares ground-truth (GT) document layouts with outputs from six different layout analysis models (DocLayNet, M6Doc, CHDAC-2022, cTDaR-modern, cTDaR-archival) across two distinct datasets: one featuring complex, multi-column Chinese documents and another featuring real-world images containing text (like phone screens, signs, and murals). For each input, the model’s prediction is shown below the GT, demonstrating varying degrees of accuracy in detecting and segmenting text blocks and their hierarchical structure.
